text: There are many more journals in political science today, and most have more papers to review than ever before. Editors worry about reviewer fatigue, the assumption being that academics will decline requests to review frequently because they will be asked too often. This paper is the first empirical analysis of the acceptance and refusal rates of reviewer requests and the reasons that academics give for their refusals. It finds that reviewer fatigue is only one of a number of factors that influence an academics' decisions about whether to accept or decline a request to review. While academics are generally willing to review in the sense that they are prepared to do the hard work involved in being a reviewer in the manner described above, there are a host of other factors that mean they are rarely in a position to do so.
